44: 	// compatible behaviour will be enforced

50: // SMTAlignmentError represents an error due to SMT alignment
51: type SMTAlignmentError struct {
52: 	RequestedCPUs         int
53: 	CpusPerCore           int
54: 	AvailablePhysicalCPUs int
55: 	CausedByPhysicalCPUs  bool
56: }
57: 
58: func (e SMTAlignmentError) Error() string {
59: 	if e.CausedByPhysicalCPUs {
60: 		return fmt.Sprintf("SMT Alignment Error: not enough free physical CPUs: available physical CPUs = %d, requested CPUs = %d, CPUs per core = %d", e.AvailablePhysicalCPUs, e.RequestedCPUs, e.CpusPerCore)
61: 	}
62: 	return fmt.Sprintf("SMT Alignment Error: requested %d cpus not multiple cpus per core = %d", e.RequestedCPUs, e.CpusPerCore)
63: }
64: 
65: // Type returns human-readable type of this error. Used in the admission control to populate Admission Failure reason.
66: func (e SMTAlignmentError) Type() string {
67: 	return ErrorSMTAlignment
68: }

70: // staticPolicy is a CPU manager policy that does not change CPU
71: // assignments for exclusively pinned guaranteed containers after the main
72: // container process starts.
73: //
74: // This policy allocates CPUs exclusively for a container if all the following
75: // conditions are met:
76: //
77: // - The pod QoS class is Guaranteed.
78: // - The CPU request is a positive integer.
79: //
80: // The static policy maintains the following sets of logical CPUs:
81: //
82: //   - SHARED: Burstable, BestEffort, and non-integral Guaranteed containers
83: //     run here. Initially this contains all CPU IDs on the system. As
84: //     exclusive allocations are created and destroyed, this CPU set shrinks
85: //     and grows, accordingly. This is stored in the state as the default
86: //     CPU set.
87: //
88: //   - RESERVED: A subset of the shared pool which is not exclusively
89: //     allocatable. The membership of this pool is static for the lifetime of
90: //     the Kubelet. The size of the reserved pool is
91: //     ceil(systemreserved.cpu + kubereserved.cpu).
92: //     Reserved CPUs are taken topologically starting with lowest-indexed
93: //     physical core, as reported by cAdvisor.
94: //
95: //   - ASSIGNABLE: Equal to SHARED - RESERVED. Exclusive CPUs are allocated
96: //     from this pool.
97: //
98: //   - EXCLUSIVE ALLOCATIONS: CPU sets assigned exclusively to one container.
99: //     These are stored as explicit assignments in the state.
100: //

53: // NewCgroupName composes a new cgroup name.

56: func NewCgroupName(base CgroupName, components ...string) CgroupName {
57: 	for _, component := range components {
58: 		// Forbit using "_" in internal names. When remapping internal
59: 		// names to systemd cgroup driver, we want to remap "-" => "_",
60: 		// so we forbid "_" so that we can always reverse the mapping.
61: 		if strings.Contains(component, "/") || strings.Contains(component, "_") {
62: 			panic(fmt.Errorf("invalid character in component [%q] of CgroupName", component))
63: 		}
64: 	}
65: 	return CgroupName(append(append([]string{}, base...), components...))

77: // For example, the name {"kubepods", "burstable", "pod1234-abcd-5678-efgh"} becomes
78: // "/kubepods.slice/kubepods-burstable.slice/kubepods-burstable-pod1234_abcd_5678_efgh.slice"
79: // This function always expands the systemd name into the cgroupfs form. If only
80: // the last part is needed, use path.Base(...) on it to discard the rest.
81: func (cgroupName CgroupName) ToSystemd() string {
82: 	if len(cgroupName) == 0 || (len(cgroupName) == 1 && cgroupName[0] == "") {
83: 		return "/"
84: 	}
85: 	newparts := []string{}
86: 	for _, part := range cgroupName {
87: 		part = escapeSystemdCgroupName(part)
88: 		newparts = append(newparts, part)
89: 	}
90: 
91: 	result, err := cgroupsystemd.ExpandSlice(strings.Join(newparts, "-") + systemdSuffix)
92: 	if err != nil {
93: 		// Should never happen...
94: 		panic(fmt.Errorf("error converting cgroup name [%v] to systemd format: %v", cgroupName, err))
95: 	}
96: 	return result
97: }

54: // Validate checks if all subsystem cgroups are valid
55: func (c *cgroupV1impl) Validate(name CgroupName) error {
56: 	// Get map of all cgroup paths on the system for the particular cgroup
57: 	cgroupPaths := c.buildCgroupPaths(name)
58: 
59: 	// the presence of alternative control groups not known to runc confuses
60: 	// the kubelet existence checks.
61: 	// ideally, we would have a mechanism in runc to support Exists() logic
62: 	// scoped to the set control groups it understands.  this is being discussed
63: 	// in https://github.com/opencontainers/runc/issues/1440
64: 	// once resolved, we can remove this code.
65: 	allowlistControllers := sets.New[string]("cpu", "cpuacct", "cpuset", "memory", "systemd", "pids")
66: 
67: 	if _, ok := c.subsystems.MountPoints["hugetlb"]; ok {
68: 		allowlistControllers.Insert("hugetlb")
69: 	}
70: 	var missingPaths []string
71: 	// If even one cgroup path doesn't exist, then the cgroup doesn't exist.
72: 	for controller, path := range cgroupPaths {
73: 		// ignore mounts we don't care about
74: 		if !allowlistControllers.Has(controller) {
75: 			continue
76: 		}
77: 		if !libcontainercgroups.PathExists(path) {
78: 			missingPaths = append(missingPaths, path)
79: 		}
80: 	}

{"content":"2504: // state every sync-frequency seconds. Never returns.
2505: func (kl *Kubelet) syncLoop(ctx context.Context, updates <-chan kubetypes.PodUpdate, handler SyncHandler) {
2506: 	logger := klog.FromContext(ctx)
2507: 	logger.Info("Starting kubelet main sync loop")
2508: 	// The syncTicker wakes up kubelet to checks if there are any pod workers
2509: 	// that need to be sync'd. A one-second period is sufficient because the
2510: 	// sync interval is defaulted to 10s.
2511: 	syncTicker := time.NewTicker(time.Second)
2512: 	defer syncTicker.Stop()
2513: 	housekeepingTicker := time.NewTicker(housekeepingPeriod)
2514: 	defer housekeepingTicker.Stop()
2515: 	plegCh := kl.pleg.Watch()
2516: 	const (
2517: 		base   = 100 * time.Millisecond
2518: 		max    = 5 * time.Second
2519: 		factor = 2
2520: 	)
2521: 	duration := base
2522: 	// Responsible for checking limits in resolv.conf
2523: 	// The limits do not have anything to do with individual pods
2524: 	// Since this is called in syncLoop, we don't need to call it anywhere else
2525: 	if kl.dnsConfigurer != nil && kl.dnsConfigurer.ResolverConfig != "" {
2526: 		kl.dnsConfigurer.CheckLimitsForResolvConf(klog.FromContext(ctx))
2527: 	}
2528: 
2529: 	for {
2530: 		if err := kl.runtimeState.runtimeErrors(); err != nil {
2531: 			logger.Error(err, "Skipping pod synchronization")
2532: 			// exponential backoff
2533: 			time.Sleep(duration)
2534: 			duration = time.Duration(math.Min(float64(max), factor*float64(duration)))
2535: 			continue
2536: 		}
2537: 		// reset backoff if we have a success
2538: 		duration = base
2539: 
2540: 		kl.syncLoopMonitor.Store(kl.clock.Now())
2541: 		if !kl.syncLoopIteration(ctx, updates, handler, syncTicker.C, housekeepingTicker.C, plegCh) {
2542: 			break
2543: 		}
2544: 		kl.syncLoopMonitor.Store(kl.clock.Now())
2545: 	}
2546: }
2547: 
2548: // syncLoopIteration reads from various channels and dispatches pods to the
2549: // given handler.
2550: //
2551: // Arguments:
2552: // 1.  configCh:       a channel to read config events from
2553: // 2.  handler:        the SyncHandler to dispatch pods to
2554: // 3.  syncCh:         a channel to read periodic sync events from
2555: // 4.  housekeepingCh: a channel to read housekeeping events from
2556: // 5.  plegCh:         a channel to read PLEG updates from
2557: //
2558: // Events are also read from the kubelet liveness manager's update channel.
2559: //
2560: // The workflow is to read from one of the channels, handle that event, and
2561: // update the timestamp in the sync loop monitor.
2562: //
2563: // Here is an appropriate place to note that despite the syntactical
2564: // similarity to the switch statement, the case statements in a select are
2565: // evaluated in a pseudorandom order if there are multiple channels ready to
2566: // read from when the select is evaluated.  In other words, case statements
2567: // are evaluated in random order, and you can not assume that the case
2568: // statements evaluate in order if multiple channels have events.
2569: //
2570: // With that in mind, in truly no particular order, the different channels
2571: // are handled as follows:
2572: //
2573: //   - configCh: dispatch the pods for the config change to the appropriate
2574: //     handler callback for the event type
2575: //   - plegCh: update the runtime cache; sync pod
2576: //   - syncCh: sync all pods waiting for sync
2577: //   - housekeepingCh: trigger cleanup of pods
2578: //   - health manager: sync pods that have failed or in which one or more

arguments.content:
# Kubelet Container Manager: Subsystem Architecture Guide

## 1. Purpose, Boundaries, and Dependencies

### Purpose

The container manager (`pkg/kubelet/cm`) is the kubelet subsystem responsible for translating Kubernetes resource semantics—CPU requests, memory limits, QoS classes, device plugins, NUMA topology—into concrete OS-level enforcement. On Linux this means writing cgroup parameters. On other platforms it provides stubs or minimal implementations. It does not schedule pods (that is the kube-scheduler's job) nor does it launch containers (that is the CRI runtime's job). Its mandate is strictly _resource boundary enforcement_ and _device/hardware allocation_.

### Subsystem Boundaries

**Upstream inputs:**
- Pod specs from the kubelet's pod manager (via `ActivePodsFunc`)
- Node hardware topology from cAdvisor (`cadvisorapi.MachineInfo`)
- Feature gates from `utilfeature.DefaultFeatureGate`
- CRI runtime service for reading current container state and updating container resources (`internalapi.RuntimeService`)
- Kubernetes API client for DRA (Dynamic Resource Allocation) claim resolution

**Downstream outputs:**
- Cgroup filesystem writes (memory limits, CPU shares/quotas, cpuset affinity, pids limits, hugetlb limits)
- `ContainerResources` updates sent to the CRI runtime via `UpdateContainerResources()`
- `RunContainerOptions` returned to `kuberuntime` containing device mounts, environment variables, and CDI device names
- A `resourceupdates.Update` channel consumed by the kubelet sync loop to re-sync pods whose device health changed

**Key external dependencies:**
- `github.com/opencontainers/cgroups` (runc libcontainer): the actual cgroup read/write operations
- `github.com/opencontainers/cgroups/systemd`: systemd cgroup name conversion
- cAdvisor for machine topology and filesystem capacity
- The kubelet lifecycle package (`pkg/kubelet/lifecycle`) for the `PodAdmitHandler` interface

---

## 2. Key Components and Responsibility Split

### 2.1 `ContainerManager` Interface (`pkg/kubelet/cm/container_manager.go`)

The top-level interface through which the rest of kubelet interacts with this subsystem. It exposes:

- **Lifecycle**: `Start()`, which initialises all sub-managers and begins background goroutines
- **Node capacity**: `GetCapacity()`, `GetNodeAllocatableReservation()`, `GetNodeAllocatableAbsolute()`
- **Cgroup hierarchy**: `NewPodContainerManager()`, `GetQOSContainersInfo()`, `UpdateQOSCgroups()`, `GetPodCgroupRoot()`
- **Resource allocation gate**: `GetResources()` (returns runtime options for a container), `UpdatePluginResources()`, `GetAllocateResourcesPodAdmitHandler()`
- **DRA**: `PrepareDynamicResources()`, `UnprepareDynamicResources()`, `PodMightNeedToUnprepareResources()`
- **PodResources API**: implements `CPUsProvider`, `DevicesProvider`, `MemoryProvider`, `DynamicResourcesProvider`
- **Event stream**: `Updates()` returns a channel for device-health-driven pod re-syncs

### 2.2 `containerManagerImpl` (Linux, `pkg/kubelet/cm/container_manager_linux.go`)

The concrete Linux implementation. It aggregates every sub-manager:

```
containerManagerImpl
├── cgroupManager         CgroupManager           (cgroup filesystem CRUD)
├── qosContainerManager   QOSContainerManager      (top-level QoS cgroups)
├── deviceManager         devicemanager.Manager    (device plugins)
├── cpuManager            cpumanager.Manager       (CPU affinity/pinning)
├── memoryManager         memorymanager.Manager    (NUMA memory affinity)
├── topologyManager       topologymanager.Manager  (cross-resource NUMA coordination)
└── draManager            *dra.Manager             (Dynamic Resource Allocation)
```

Platform variants:
- `container_manager_windows.go` (`//go:build windows`): limited implementation with device manager, CPU manager, and memory manager, but no cgroup hierarchy.
- `container_manager_stub.go`: no-op implementation for tests and unsupported platforms.
- `container_manager_unsupported.go`: compile-time stub for unsupported OS.

### 2.3 `CgroupManager` Interface (`pkg/kubelet/cm/types.go`)

Abstracts all cgroup filesystem operations. Two concrete implementations:
- `cgroupV1impl` (`cgroup_v1_manager_linux.go`): uses runc libcontainer's v1 manager; validates against an allowlist of `cpu`, `cpuacct`, `cpuset`, `memory`, `systemd`, `pids`, `hugetlb`.
- `cgroupV2impl` (`cgroup_v2_manager_linux.go`): unified hierarchy using runc's cgroupv2 manager.

Both are selected at runtime by `NewCgroupManager()` which calls `cgroupManager.Version()` to record whether the host uses v1 or v2. The `NodeConfig.CgroupDriver` field selects between raw cgroupfs paths and systemd slice names.

**Name conversion**: The internal `CgroupName` type (a `[]string`, e.g. `{"kubepods", "burstable", "pod<uid>"}`) is converted to systemd's nested slice naming (`/kubepods.slice/kubepods-burstable.slice/kubepods-burstable-pod<uid>.slice`) by `CgroupName.ToSystemd()` in `cgroup_manager_linux.go:81`.

### 2.4 `QOSContainerManager` (`pkg/kubelet/cm/qos_container_manager_linux.go`)

Creates and maintains the three-tier cgroup hierarchy under the `kubepods` root:

```
/kubepods
├── /kubepods/burstable
└── /kubepods/besteffort
(Guaranteed pods land directly under /kubepods)
```

On startup it creates or updates the `burstable` and `besteffort` cgroups. It then runs a background goroutine (`periodicQOSCgroupUpdateInterval = 1 minute`) calling `UpdateCgroups()`, which:
1. Sums CPU requests across all Burstable pods → sets `cpu.shares` on the burstable cgroup
2. Ensures BestEffort stays at the kernel minimum (2 shares)
3. If `MemoryQoS` feature gate is on and cgroup v2 is in use, sets `memory.min` for memory protection
4. If `QOSReserved` feature gate is on, computes memory limits by tier and calls `retrySetMemoryReserve()` if the initial write fails (because current usage exceeds the desired limit)

### 2.5 `PodContainerManager` (`pkg/kubelet/cm/types.go`, implemented in `pod_container_manager_linux.go`)

Manages the per-pod cgroup that sits beneath the QoS tier cgroup. Two implementations:

- `podContainerManagerImpl` (when `CgroupsPerQOS = true`): creates `pod<uid>` cgroups under the appropriate QoS cgroup; enforces CPU CFS quota, memory limits, pids limits, and MemoryQoS `memory.min`/`memory.high`; handles cgroup cleanup by killing all processes then deleting the cgroup.
- `podContainerManagerNoop` (when `CgroupsPerQOS = false`): all methods are no-ops; `Exists()` always returns true.

`GetPodContainerName()` determines the pod's position in the hierarchy by calling `v1qos.GetPodQOS(pod)` and constructing a `CgroupName` whose parent is the appropriate QoS cgroup root.

### 2.6 `NodeContainerManager` (functions in `node_container_manager_linux.go`)

Handles the `kubepods` cgroup itself (the "node allocatable" enforcement boundary). Key responsibilities:
- `createNodeAllocatableCgroups()`: creates the `kubepods` root cgroup if missing
- `enforceNodeAllocatableCgroups()`: applies computed resource limits to `kubepods`; spawns a retry goroutine (sleeping 1 minute between attempts) if the first `cgroupManager.Update()` call fails
- `enforceExistingCgroup()`: enforces user-specified `KubeReserved` and `SystemReserved` limits on their respective cgroups
- `GetNodeAllocatableReservation()`: computes `systemReserved + kubeReserved + hardEvictionThresholds`
- `GetNodeAllocatableAbsolute()`: computes `capacity - reservation`

### 2.7 `InternalContainerLifecycle` (`pkg/kubelet/cm/internal_container_lifecycle.go`)

A narrow interface called by `kuberuntime` at specific container lifecycle points:

- `PreCreateContainer()` (Linux only, `internal_container_lifecycle_linux.go`): injects CPU affinity (`cpuset.cpus`) from the CPU manager and NUMA memory affinity (`cpuset.mems`) from the memory manager directly into the CRI `ContainerConfig` before the container is created.
- `PreStartContainer()`: registers the container with the CPU manager (`AddContainer`), memory manager (`AddContainer`), and topology manager (`AddContainer`).
- `PostStopContainer()`: deregisters from the topology manager (`RemoveContainer`).

### 2.8 CPU Manager (`pkg/kubelet/cm/cpumanager/`)

Manages exclusive CPU pinning for Guaranteed QoS containers with integer CPU requests. Two policies:

- **None policy** (`policy_none.go`): no-op; all containers share the full CPU set.
- **Static policy** (`policy_static.go`): maintains three CPU sets:
  - `SHARED`: default pool for non-pinned containers
  - `RESERVED`: ceil(systemReserved + kubeReserved CPUs), always in the shared pool, never exclusively allocatable
  - `EXCLUSIVE`: per-container allocations for Guaranteed pods with integer CPU requests

  On allocation it calls `takeByTopology()` in `cpu_assignment.go` to select CPUs preferring NUMA-local and SMT-aligned groupings. The selected CPUs are removed from SHARED and stored in the checkpoint state.

State is persisted to `cpu_manager_state` checkpoint file in `KubeletRootDir`. On start, the manager reconciles its persisted state against running containers by calling `UpdateContainerResources()` on the CRI runtime to re-apply `cpuset.cpus` settings. The reconciliation loop runs every `CPUManagerReconcilePeriod`.

### 2.9 Memory Manager (`pkg/kubelet/cm/memorymanager/`)

Manages NUMA-aware memory allocation with two policies:
- **None**: no-op.
- **Static**: tracks available memory per NUMA node from machine topology; assigns Guaranteed pods to specific NUMA nodes and sets `cpuset.mems` in the container's cgroup.

Like the CPU manager it uses a file-backed checkpoint and reconciles on startup.

### 2.10 Topology Manager (`pkg/kubelet/cm/topologymanager/`)

A coordination layer that collects NUMA affinity _hints_ from all registered `HintProvider`s (CPU manager, memory manager, device manager) and produces a consensus optimal NUMA affinity before any allocation is committed.

**Policies** (`policy_*.go`):
- `none`: accepts all allocations
- `best-effort`: prefers NUMA-aligned allocations but does not reject on mismatch
- `restricted`: rejects pods that cannot be NUMA-aligned
- `single-numa-node`: requires all resources to fit on a single NUMA node

**Scopes** (`scope_container.go`, `scope_pod.go`):
- Container scope: runs the hint-merge algorithm per container
- Pod scope: runs it once across all containers in the pod

The topology manager acts as a `PodAdmitHandler` (registered at `kubelet.go:1059` via `GetAllocateResourcesPodAdmitHandler()`). During pod admission it calls `Admit()` which triggers `GetTopologyHints()` on each hint provider, merges the results, and then calls `Allocate()` on each provider with the agreed affinity stored in its `Store`. Hard limit: `defaultMaxAllowableNUMANodes = 8`; more NUMA nodes cause an error at manager construction time.

### 2.11 Device Manager (`pkg/kubelet/cm/devicemanager/`)

Manages device plugin registrations and allocations. The `ManagerImpl`:
- Runs a gRPC server that device plugin DaemonSets register against (via the plugin watcher)
- Maintains sets of healthy and unhealthy devices reported by each plugin
- Checkpoints allocations to `checkpoint/` under `KubeletRootDir` to survive kubelet restarts
- Implements `HintProvider` so topology manager can factor device topology into NUMA alignment
- Exposes a health update channel (`update chan resourceupdates.Update`); when a device changes health state it sends pod UIDs that need re-syncing

The device manager's `GetDeviceRunContainerOptions()` returns the mounts, environment variables, and CDI device IDs required by the container's allocated devices; this is called from `containerManagerImpl.GetResources()`.

### 2.12 DRA Manager (`pkg/kubelet/cm/dra/`)

Implements Dynamic Resource Allocation (feature-gated by `DynamicResourceAllocation`). Communicates with DRA drivers (DaemonSets that register via the DRA plugin API) to `PrepareResources()` and `UnprepareResources()` for pods. On kubelet restart it may need to unprepare resources for pods that were never cleaned up, detectable via `PodMightNeedToUnprepareResources()`.

---

## 3. End-to-End Flow

### 3.1 Startup Sequence

`NewContainerManager()` in `container_manager_linux.go:207`:

1. Calls `GetCgroupSubsystems()` to enumerate mounted cgroup controllers.
2. Detects swap state; emits warnings if swap is on and `tmpfs noswap` is unsupported.
3. Queries cAdvisor for `MachineInfo` (CPU topology, NUMA nodes, memory) and `pidlimit.Stats()`.
4. Constructs `CgroupManager` (v1 or v2, cgroupfs or systemd driver).
5. Validates `cgroupRoot` exists; appends `defaultNodeAllocatableCgroupName` (`kubepods`) to get the actual pod root.
6. Creates `QOSContainerManager`.
7. Creates `topologymanager.Manager`.
8. Creates `devicemanager.ManagerImpl`; registers it as a topology hint provider.
9. Optionally creates `dra.Manager`; registers DRA metrics collector.
10. Creates `cpumanager.Manager`; registers it as a topology hint provider.
11. Creates `memorymanager.Manager`; registers it as a topology hint provider.
12. Starts fan-in goroutines to multiplex update channels from device manager and DRA manager into a single `resourceUpdates` channel.

`ContainerManager.Start()` in `container_manager_linux.go:615`:

1. Builds `containerMap` from the CRI runtime to identify pre-existing containers.
2. Starts DRA manager.
3. Starts CPU manager (loads checkpoint, reconciles existing containers, starts reconcile loop).
4. Starts memory manager (same pattern).
5. Caches node info; fetches rootfs capacity from cAdvisor if local storage isolation is on.
6. Validates node allocatable configuration.
7. Calls `setupNode()`:
   a. Validates required cgroup subsystems exist (`validateSystemRequirements()`).
   b. Sets kernel tunables (`vm.overcommit_memory`, `vm.panic_on_oom`, `kernel.panic`, `kernel.panic_on_oops`, keyring limits) based on `ProtectKernelDefaults`.
   c. Creates and starts QOS container hierarchy if `CgroupsPerQOS`.
   d. Enforces node allocatable cgroups.
   e. Sets up `systemContainers` for the runtime and kubelet itself.
8. Starts periodic goroutines for `ensureStateFunc` (every 1 minute) and any periodic tasks (every 5 minutes).
9. Starts device manager (begins serving device plugin gRPC, loads checkpoints, reconciles containers).

### 3.2 Pod Admission Flow

When a new pod arrives and passes through kubelet's admit handlers (`kubelet.go:1059`):

```
lifecycle.PodAdmitHandler chain
└── containerManager.GetAllocateResourcesPodAdmitHandler()
    └── topologymanager.Manager.Admit()
        ├── For each container in pod:
        │   ├── cpuManager.GetTopologyHints()
        │   ├── memoryManager.GetTopologyHints()
        │   └── deviceManager.GetTopologyHints()
        ├── Merge hints → best NUMA affinity bitmask
        └── For each container:
            ├── cpuManager.Allocate()    → reserves CPUs in state
            ├── memoryManager.Allocate() → reserves NUMA memory blocks
            └── deviceManager.Allocate() → calls device plugin Allocate RPC
```

Additionally `lifecycle.NewPredicateAdmitHandler` calls `containerManager.UpdatePluginResources()`, which delegates to the device manager to update the scheduler's node info with current allocatable device capacity.

### 3.3 Container Launch Flow

When `kuberuntime` creates a container it calls through `InternalContainerLifecycle`:

```
kuberuntime.createContainer()
├── internalContainerLifecycle.PreCreateContainer()
│   ├── cpuManager.GetCPUAffinity() → inject cpuset.cpus into ContainerConfig
│   └── memoryManager.GetMemoryNUMANodes() → inject cpuset.mems into ContainerConfig
│
├── containerManager.GetResources()
│   ├── draManager.GetResources() → CDI device IDs for DRA
│   └── deviceManager.GetDeviceRunContainerOptions() → mounts, env vars, CDI devices
│       (allocation was already done at admission; this reads cached state)
│
├── CRI: CreateContainer(ContainerConfig with cpuset.cpus, cpuset.mems, devices, mounts)
│
└── internalContainerLifecycle.PreStartContainer()
    ├── cpuManager.AddContainer() → records containerID → (pod, container) mapping
    ├── memoryManager.AddContainer() → same
    └── topologyManager.AddContainer() → tracks for hint store
```

### 3.4 Pod Cgroup Lifecycle

Pod workers call `PodContainerManager.EnsureExists()` before running any containers:

```
podContainerManagerImpl.EnsureExists()
├── Determine QoS class → parent cgroup path
├── Build ResourceConfig from pod spec:
│   ├── ResourceConfigForPod() → CPU shares, CFS quota, memory limit
│   ├── PidsLimit if configured
│   └── MemoryQoS unified fields (memory.min, memory.high) if cgroup v2 + feature gate
└── cgroupManager.Create()
```

On pod termination, pod workers call `PodContainerManager.Destroy()`:

```
podContainerManagerImpl.Destroy()
├── tryKillingCgroupProcesses() → kill all PIDs in cgroup (up to 5 retries)
└── cgroupManager.Destroy()    → rmdir the cgroup
```

### 3.5 Device Health Update Loop

When a device plugin reports a device health change, the device manager sends to its internal `update` channel. A fan-in goroutine in `NewContainerManager` (`container_manager_linux.go:375`) forwards this to `cm.resourceUpdates`. The kubelet sync loop reads from `containerManager.Updates()` (`kubelet.go:2665`) and calls `handler.HandlePodSyncs()` for affected pods, causing a fresh status update that reflects the new device health.

---

## 4. Failure Modes, Limits, and Design Tradeoffs

### 4.1 Failure Modes

**Cgroup write failures on node allocatable enforcement**: `enforceNodeAllocatableCgroups()` (`node_container_manager_linux.go:95`) spawns a background goroutine that retries `cgroupManager.Update()` indefinitely with 1-minute sleeps, emitting Kubernetes Warning events on each failure. This prevents a hard startup failure when existing pod memory usage temporarily exceeds the new limit.

**QoS cgroup update failures**: `qosContainerManagerImpl.UpdateCgroups()` (`qos_container_manager_linux.go:316`) attempts a `retrySetMemoryReserve()` fallback that caps the limit to current memory usage when the first write is rejected. If updates still fail, the error is logged but the goroutine continues; QoS limits may drift from desired state until the next 1-minute reconcile.

**CPU manager checkpoint corruption**: If `state.NewCheckpointState()` fails, the CPU manager refuses to start and the kubelet returns an error. The operator must drain the node and remove the state file. This is intentional—silently starting with corrupt state would produce incorrect CPU pinning.

**Device plugin crashes**: The device manager marks devices from a crashed plugin as unhealthy and sends updates via the health channel. Pods using those devices get re-synced; the kubelet emits events. The checkpoint retains allocations so that if the plugin reconnects before the pod is deleted, allocation state can be reconciled.

**NUMA nodes > 8**: `topology_manager.go:41` defines `defaultMaxAllowableNUMANodes = 8`. Exceeding this causes `topologymanager.NewManager()` to return an error, preventing kubelet startup with topology manager enabled on large multi-socket machines.

**Swap enabled with `failSwapOn=true`**: `NewContainerManager` returns an error immediately, preventing kubelet from starting. With `failSwapOn=false`, the kubelet continues but emits a warning event about memory-backed volumes potentially being swapped to disk.

**cgroup v1 on new kernels**: A deprecation warning (`CgroupV1DeprecatedWarning`) is defined in `container_manager.go:52`. cgroup v1 support is intended for removal in a future release.

### 4.2 Design Tradeoffs

**Two-phase admission/allocation**: The topology manager's `Admit()` phase calls `Allocate()` on hint providers before the container is created. This means device allocations are committed at pod admission time, not at container start time. If the container never starts (e.g., image pull failure), the device remains allocated until the kubelet cleans up the pod.

**Checkpoint-based state persistence**: CPU, memory, and device managers all use checkpoint files under `KubeletRootDir`. This allows kubelet restarts without losing allocations. The downside is that checkpoint format changes require migration logic and draining.

**Synchronous QoS hierarchy updates**: The QoS cgroup update goroutine holds a mutex for its entire update cycle. During that time, new pod admissions that need `UpdateQOSCgroups()` must wait. This is acceptable because the update is fast (a few cgroup writes) and happens only once per minute.

**Noop implementations as a compile-time seam**: The `containerManagerStub` (`container_manager_stub.go`) and `podContainerManagerNoop` provide full no-op implementations of the interfaces. This decouples the kubelet's main path from the Linux-specific cgroup machinery at the cost of maintaining two parallel code paths.

---

## 5. Extension Points, Customisation Hooks, and Risks

### 5.1 CPU Manager Policy

`cpumanager.Policy` is an interface (`policy.go`) with `Start()`, `Allocate()`, `RemoveContainer()`, `GetTopologyHints()`, `GetAllocatableCPUs()`. New policies can be registered by adding a `case` to the `switch` in `NewManager()`. Policy options (`CPUManagerPolicyOptions map[string]string` in `NodeConfig`) allow feature flags per policy without changing the interface.

The static policy has options including `full-pcpus-only` (require whole physical cores), `distribute-cpus-across-numa`, `distribute-cpus-across-cores`, and `align-by-socket`. These are parsed in `policy_options.go` and change the CPU assignment topology.

**Risk**: A buggy policy can silently mis-assign CPUs or fail to clean up exclusive allocations; the CPU set leaked from a terminated container remains unavailable for new ones until the reconciler detects the container is gone.

### 5.2 Topology Manager Policy and Scope

Both `TopologyManagerPolicy` and `TopologyManagerScope` are strings from `NodeConfig`. Adding a new policy requires adding a `policy_*.go` file and registering it in `topology_manager.go`. The scope (container vs. pod) changes whether hints are merged per-container or across all containers at once.

**Risk**: `best-effort` silently allows non-optimal NUMA placement; operators may not notice increased cross-NUMA memory traffic until they measure latency. `restricted` and `single-numa-node` can cause admission failures that are opaque to end users if hint providers cannot agree.

### 5.3 Device Plugins

Device plugins register via the plugin watcher (`GetPluginRegistrationHandlers()` returns a map including `DevicePlugin` → `deviceManager.GetWatcherHandler()`). Any DaemonSet can register additional resource types without modifying kubelet code. The device manager's `Allocate()` delegates entirely to the plugin's gRPC response.

**Risk**: A device plugin that returns inconsistent topology information will cause the topology manager to make suboptimal NUMA placements. A plugin that crashes at `Allocate()` time causes pod admission to fail; kubelet will retry the admission on the next sync cycle.

### 5.4 DRA Drivers

DRA drivers register via the DRA plugin watcher (`DRAPlugin` key in `GetPluginRegistrationHandlers()`). The `dra.Manager` communicates with the driver to `PrepareResources()`, writing CDI device annotations that are passed to the CRI runtime. DRA is gated by `DynamicResourceAllocation` feature gate.

**Risk**: DRA state is stored in `ResourceClaim` objects in the API server and locally in the DRA manager's state directory. Inconsistencies between these two sources of truth on kubelet restart require the `PodMightNeedToUnprepareResources()` / `UnprepareResources()` recovery path; if the driver itself is unavailable at unprepare time, resources may leak.

### 5.5 MemoryQoS (cgroup v2 only)

Controlled by the `MemoryQoS` feature gate. When enabled, `memory.min` is set on QoS cgroups and pod cgroups to provide memory protection guarantees. The `Unified` map field in `ResourceConfig` carries arbitrary cgroup v2 key-value pairs, providing an extension point for future cgroup v2 features without changing the `ResourceConfig` struct.

### 5.6 PodLevelResources

The `qos_container_manager_linux.go:188` references `kubefeatures.PodLevelResources` to decide whether to skip pod-level resource aggregation when computing Burstable CPU shares. This is an example of a feature gate that changes the aggregation semantics within an existing component.

---

## 6. Operational Implications

### 6.1 Performance

**CPU manager reconciliation**: The reconcile loop calls `UpdateContainerResources()` on the CRI runtime for every container whose CPU set has changed since the last reconcile. On a node with hundreds of containers and the static policy, this can produce bursts of gRPC calls. The reconcile period (`CPUManagerReconcilePeriod`, default 10 seconds) controls this tradeoff.

**QoS cgroup update cost**: Every minute, `UpdateCgroups()` iterates all active pods to sum CPU requests. On nodes with many pods, this is O(pods × containers). The update itself is a handful of cgroup writes, but the pod iteration under lock can delay admission.

**Cgroup write latency on systemd**: When using the systemd cgroup driver, `cgroup_manager_linux.go` converts every cgroup name to a systemd slice path. Systemd must be notified via D-Bus for each cgroup operation. On high-density nodes this can become a bottleneck; the cgroupfs driver avoids this overhead.

### 6.2 Reliability

**Kubelet restart resilience**: CPU, memory, and device managers write state to checkpoint files before returning success from `Allocate()`. On restart, `Start()` reads the checkpoint and calls `UpdateContainerResources()` for any container still running to restore affinity. This means brief affinity drift (the container runs on the wrong CPUs) during the window between kubelet crash and restart recovery.

**cgroup hierarchy divergence**: The QoS and pod container managers are responsible for creating and destroying cgroups in sync with pod lifecycle. If the kubelet crashes between creating a pod cgroup and launching the container, orphan cgroups may exist. `GetAllPodsFromCgroups()` on `podContainerManagerImpl` scans the filesystem to enumerate orphan pod cgroups, which `HandlePodCleanups()` uses for garbage collection.

**`ShouldResetExtendedResourceCapacity()`**: Called on kubelet startup via `kubelet_resources.go`. The device manager returns `true` if the checkpoint indicates the node identity changed (e.g., node was recreated). In that case the kubelet zeroes extended resource capacity in the node status, forcing device plugins to re-register.

### 6.3 Maintainability

**cgroup v1 deprecation**: `container_manager.go:52` contains the deprecation warning. The v1-specific code paths in `cgroup_v1_manager_linux.go` and the v1 branches in `helpers_linux.go` represent ongoing maintenance burden. Removal requires coordinated changes across multiple files.

**`klog.TODO()` debt**: Several methods in `container_manager_linux.go` (e.g., `PodHasExclusiveCPUs`, `GetCapacity`) call `klog.TODO()` because they lack a proper context. This is flagged in source comments as a known issue requiring refactoring to pass loggers through more of the call chain.

**Mockery-generated mocks**: The `//go:generate mockery` directive in `container_manager.go` generates mocks for testing. The `.mockery.yaml` controls which interfaces are mocked. This keeps test doubles in sync with the interface, but any interface change requires running `go generate` and committing updated mock files.

---

## 7. File and Directory Map

| Path | Role |
|------|------|
| `pkg/kubelet/cm/container_manager.go` | `ContainerManager` interface, `NodeConfig`, `NodeAllocatableConfig`, helper functions |
| `pkg/kubelet/cm/container_manager_linux.go` | Linux `containerManagerImpl`: construction, `Start()`, resource delegation |
| `pkg/kubelet/cm/container_manager_windows.go` | Windows `containerManagerImpl`: capacity and device management only |
| `pkg/kubelet/cm/container_manager_stub.go` | No-op stub for tests and unsupported platforms |
| `pkg/kubelet/cm/types.go` | `CgroupManager`, `PodContainerManager`, `QOSContainersInfo`, `ResourceConfig`, `CgroupName` |
| `pkg/kubelet/cm/cgroup_manager_linux.go` | Name conversion (cgroupfs ↔ systemd), `NewCgroupManager()`, common cgroup manager |
| `pkg/kubelet/cm/cgroup_v1_manager_linux.go` | cgroup v1 implementation (`cgroupV1impl`) |
| `pkg/kubelet/cm/cgroup_v2_manager_linux.go` | cgroup v2 implementation (`cgroupV2impl`) |
| `pkg/kubelet/cm/qos_container_manager_linux.go` | QoS tier cgroups, periodic CPU/memory updates |
| `pkg/kubelet/cm/pod_container_manager_linux.go` | Per-pod cgroup lifecycle, `podContainerManagerImpl` |
| `pkg/kubelet/cm/node_container_manager_linux.go` | `kubepods` root cgroup, node allocatable enforcement, kube/system reserved |
| `pkg/kubelet/cm/internal_container_lifecycle.go` | `InternalContainerLifecycle` interface and base implementation |
| `pkg/kubelet/cm/internal_container_lifecycle_linux.go` | Linux `PreCreateContainer`: injects cpuset affinity into CRI config |
| `pkg/kubelet/cm/helpers_linux.go` | `MilliCPUToShares()`, `MilliCPUToQuota()`, `ResourceConfigForPod()`, `HugePageLimits()` |
| `pkg/kubelet/cm/resourceupdates/updates.go` | `Update` struct for device-health-driven pod re-syncs |
| `pkg/kubelet/cm/cpumanager/cpu_manager.go` | `cpumanager.Manager` interface and `manager` struct |
| `pkg/kubelet/cm/cpumanager/policy_static.go` | Static CPU pinning policy |

# Kubelet Container Manager: Subsystem Architecture Guide

## 1. Purpose, Boundaries, and Dependencies

### Purpose

The container manager (`pkg/kubelet/cm`) is the kubelet subsystem responsible for translating Kubernetes resource semantics—CPU requests, memory limits, QoS classes, device plugins, NUMA topology—into concrete OS-level enforcement. On Linux this means writing cgroup parameters. On other platforms it provides stubs or minimal implementations. It does not schedule pods (that is the kube-scheduler's job) nor does it launch containers (that is the CRI runtime's job). Its mandate is strictly _resource boundary enforcement_ and _device/hardware allocation_.

### Subsystem Boundaries

**Upstream inputs:**
- Pod specs from the kubelet's pod manager (via `ActivePodsFunc`)
- Node hardware topology from cAdvisor (`cadvisorapi.MachineInfo`)
- Feature gates from `utilfeature.DefaultFeatureGate`
- CRI runtime service for reading current container state and updating container resources (`internalapi.RuntimeService`)
- Kubernetes API client for DRA (Dynamic Resource Allocation) claim resolution

**Downstream outputs:**
- Cgroup filesystem writes (memory limits, CPU shares/quotas, cpuset affinity, pids limits, hugetlb limits)
- `ContainerResources` updates sent to the CRI runtime via `UpdateContainerResources()`
- `RunContainerOptions` returned to `kuberuntime` containing device mounts, environment variables, and CDI device names
- A `resourceupdates.Update` channel consumed by the kubelet sync loop to re-sync pods whose device health changed

**Key external dependencies:**
- `github.com/opencontainers/cgroups` (runc libcontainer): the actual cgroup read/write operations
- `github.com/opencontainers/cgroups/systemd`: systemd cgroup name conversion
- cAdvisor for machine topology and filesystem capacity
- The kubelet lifecycle package (`pkg/kubelet/lifecycle`) for the `PodAdmitHandler` interface

---

## 2. Key Components and Responsibility Split

### 2.1 `ContainerManager` Interface (`pkg/kubelet/cm/container_manager.go`)

The top-level interface through which the rest of kubelet interacts with this subsystem. It exposes:

- **Lifecycle**: `Start()`, which initialises all sub-managers and begins background goroutines
- **Node capacity**: `GetCapacity()`, `GetNodeAllocatableReservation()`, `GetNodeAllocatableAbsolute()`
- **Cgroup hierarchy**: `NewPodContainerManager()`, `GetQOSContainersInfo()`, `UpdateQOSCgroups()`, `GetPodCgroupRoot()`
- **Resource allocation gate**: `GetResources()` (returns runtime options for a container), `UpdatePluginResources()`, `GetAllocateResourcesPodAdmitHandler()`
- **DRA**: `PrepareDynamicResources()`, `UnprepareDynamicResources()`, `PodMightNeedToUnprepareResources()`
- **PodResources API**: implements `CPUsProvider`, `DevicesProvider`, `MemoryProvider`, `DynamicResourcesProvider`
- **Event stream**: `Updates()` returns a channel for device-health-driven pod re-syncs

### 2.2 `containerManagerImpl` (Linux, `pkg/kubelet/cm/container_manager_linux.go`)

The concrete Linux implementation. It aggregates every sub-manager:

```
containerManagerImpl
├── cgroupManager         CgroupManager           (cgroup filesystem CRUD)
├── qosContainerManager   QOSContainerManager      (top-level QoS cgroups)
├── deviceManager         devicemanager.Manager    (device plugins)
├── cpuManager            cpumanager.Manager       (CPU affinity/pinning)
├── memoryManager         memorymanager.Manager    (NUMA memory affinity)
├── topologyManager       topologymanager.Manager  (cross-resource NUMA coordination)
└── draManager            *dra.Manager             (Dynamic Resource Allocation)
```

Platform variants:
- `container_manager_windows.go` (`//go:build windows`): limited implementation with device manager, CPU manager, and memory manager, but no cgroup hierarchy.
- `container_manager_stub.go`: no-op implementation for tests and unsupported platforms.
- `container_manager_unsupported.go`: compile-time stub for unsupported OS.

### 2.3 `CgroupManager` Interface (`pkg/kubelet/cm/types.go`)

Abstracts all cgroup filesystem operations. Two concrete implementations:
- `cgroupV1impl` (`cgroup_v1_manager_linux.go`): uses runc libcontainer's v1 manager; validates against an allowlist of `cpu`, `cpuacct`, `cpuset`, `memory`, `systemd`, `pids`, `hugetlb`.
- `cgroupV2impl` (`cgroup_v2_manager_linux.go`): unified hierarchy using runc's cgroupv2 manager.

Both are selected at runtime by `NewCgroupManager()` which calls `cgroupManager.Version()` to record whether the host uses v1 or v2. The `NodeConfig.CgroupDriver` field selects between raw cgroupfs paths and systemd slice names.

**Name conversion**: The internal `CgroupName` type (a `[]string`, e.g. `{"kubepods", "burstable", "pod<uid>"}`) is converted to systemd's nested slice naming (`/kubepods.slice/kubepods-burstable.slice/kubepods-burstable-pod<uid>.slice`) by `CgroupName.ToSystemd()` in `cgroup_manager_linux.go:81`.

### 2.4 `QOSContainerManager` (`pkg/kubelet/cm/qos_container_manager_linux.go`)

Creates and maintains the three-tier cgroup hierarchy under the `kubepods` root:

```
/kubepods
├── /kubepods/burstable
└── /kubepods/besteffort
(Guaranteed pods land directly under /kubepods)
```

On startup it creates or updates the `burstable` and `besteffort` cgroups. It then runs a background goroutine (`periodicQOSCgroupUpdateInterval = 1 minute`) calling `UpdateCgroups()`, which:
1. Sums CPU requests across all Burstable pods → sets `cpu.shares` on the burstable cgroup
2. Ensures BestEffort stays at the kernel minimum (2 shares)
3. If `MemoryQoS` feature gate is on and cgroup v2 is in use, sets `memory.min` for memory protection
4. If `QOSReserved` feature gate is on, computes memory limits by tier and calls `retrySetMemoryReserve()` if the initial write fails (because current usage exceeds the desired limit)

### 2.5 `PodContainerManager` (`pkg/kubelet/cm/types.go`, implemented in `pod_container_manager_linux.go`)

Manages the per-pod cgroup that sits beneath the QoS tier cgroup. Two implementations:

- `podContainerManagerImpl` (when `CgroupsPerQOS = true`): creates `pod<uid>` cgroups under the appropriate QoS cgroup; enforces CPU CFS quota, memory limits, pids limits, and MemoryQoS `memory.min`/`memory.high`; handles cgroup cleanup by killing all processes then deleting the cgroup.
- `podContainerManagerNoop` (when `CgroupsPerQOS = false`): all methods are no-ops; `Exists()` always returns true.

`GetPodContainerName()` determines the pod's position in the hierarchy by calling `v1qos.GetPodQOS(pod)` and constructing a `CgroupName` whose parent is the appropriate QoS cgroup root.

### 2.6 `NodeContainerManager` (functions in `node_container_manager_linux.go`)

Handles the `kubepods` cgroup itself (the "node allocatable" enforcement boundary). Key responsibilities:
- `createNodeAllocatableCgroups()`: creates the `kubepods` root cgroup if missing
- `enforceNodeAllocatableCgroups()`: applies computed resource limits to `kubepods`; spawns a retry goroutine (sleeping 1 minute between attempts) if the first `cgroupManager.Update()` call fails
- `enforceExistingCgroup()`: enforces user-specified `KubeReserved` and `SystemReserved` limits on their respective cgroups
- `GetNodeAllocatableReservation()`: computes `systemReserved + kubeReserved + hardEvictionThresholds`
- `GetNodeAllocatableAbsolute()`: computes `capacity - reservation`

### 2.7 `InternalContainerLifecycle` (`pkg/kubelet/cm/internal_container_lifecycle.go`)

A narrow interface called by `kuberuntime` at specific container lifecycle points:

- `PreCreateContainer()` (Linux only, `internal_container_lifecycle_linux.go`): injects CPU affinity (`cpuset.cpus`) from the CPU manager and NUMA memory affinity (`cpuset.mems`) from the memory manager directly into the CRI `ContainerConfig` before the container is created.
- `PreStartContainer()`: registers the container with the CPU manager (`AddContainer`), memory manager (`AddContainer`), and topology manager (`AddContainer`).
- `PostStopContainer()`: deregisters from the topology manager (`RemoveContainer`).

### 2.8 CPU Manager (`pkg/kubelet/cm/cpumanager/`)

Manages exclusive CPU pinning for Guaranteed QoS containers with integer CPU requests. Two policies:

- **None policy** (`policy_none.go`): no-op; all containers share the full CPU set.
- **Static policy** (`policy_static.go`): maintains three CPU sets:
  - `SHARED`: default pool for non-pinned containers
  - `RESERVED`: ceil(systemReserved + kubeReserved CPUs), always in the shared pool, never exclusively allocatable
  - `EXCLUSIVE`: per-container allocations for Guaranteed pods with integer CPU requests

  On allocation it calls `takeByTopology()` in `cpu_assignment.go` to select CPUs preferring NUMA-local and SMT-aligned groupings. The selected CPUs are removed from SHARED and stored in the checkpoint state.

State is persisted to `cpu_manager_state` checkpoint file in `KubeletRootDir`. On start, the manager reconciles its persisted state against running containers by calling `UpdateContainerResources()` on the CRI runtime to re-apply `cpuset.cpus` settings. The reconciliation loop runs every `CPUManagerReconcilePeriod`.

### 2.9 Memory Manager (`pkg/kubelet/cm/memorymanager/`)

Manages NUMA-aware memory allocation with two policies:
- **None**: no-op.
- **Static**: tracks available memory per NUMA node from machine topology; assigns Guaranteed pods to specific NUMA nodes and sets `cpuset.mems` in the container's cgroup.

Like the CPU manager it uses a file-backed checkpoint and reconciles on startup.

### 2.10 Topology Manager (`pkg/kubelet/cm/topologymanager/`)

A coordination layer that collects NUMA affinity _hints_ from all registered `HintProvider`s (CPU manager, memory manager, device manager) and produces a consensus optimal NUMA affinity before any allocation is committed.

**Policies** (`policy_*.go`):
- `none`: accepts all allocations
- `best-effort`: prefers NUMA-aligned allocations but does not reject on mismatch
- `restricted`: rejects pods that cannot be NUMA-aligned
- `single-numa-node`: requires all resources to fit on a single NUMA node

**Scopes** (`scope_container.go`, `scope_pod.go`):
- Container scope: runs the hint-merge algorithm per container
- Pod scope: runs it once across all containers in the pod

The topology manager acts as a `PodAdmitHandler` (registered at `kubelet.go:1059` via `GetAllocateResourcesPodAdmitHandler()`). During pod admission it calls `Admit()` which triggers `GetTopologyHints()` on each hint provider, merges the results, and then calls `Allocate()` on each provider with the agreed affinity stored in its `Store`. Hard limit: `defaultMaxAllowableNUMANodes = 8`; more NUMA nodes cause an error at manager construction time.

### 2.11 Device Manager (`pkg/kubelet/cm/devicemanager/`)

Manages device plugin registrations and allocations. The `ManagerImpl`:
- Runs a gRPC server that device plugin DaemonSets register against (via the plugin watcher)
- Maintains sets of healthy and unhealthy devices reported by each plugin
- Checkpoints allocations to `checkpoint/` under `KubeletRootDir` to survive kubelet restarts
- Implements `HintProvider` so topology manager can factor device topology into NUMA alignment
- Exposes a health update channel (`update chan resourceupdates.Update`); when a device changes health state it sends pod UIDs that need re-syncing

The device manager's `GetDeviceRunContainerOptions()` returns the mounts, environment variables, and CDI device IDs required by the container's allocated devices; this is called from `containerManagerImpl.GetResources()`.

### 2.12 DRA Manager (`pkg/kubelet/cm/dra/`)

Implements Dynamic Resource Allocation (feature-gated by `DynamicResourceAllocation`). Communicates with DRA drivers (DaemonSets that register via the DRA plugin API) to `PrepareResources()` and `UnprepareResources()` for pods. On kubelet restart it may need to unprepare resources for pods that were never cleaned up, detectable via `PodMightNeedToUnprepareResources()`.

---

## 3. End-to-End Flow

### 3.1 Startup Sequence

`NewContainerManager()` in `container_manager_linux.go:207`:

1. Calls `GetCgroupSubsystems()` to enumerate mounted cgroup controllers.
2. Detects swap state; emits warnings if swap is on and `tmpfs noswap` is unsupported.
3. Queries cAdvisor for `MachineInfo` (CPU topology, NUMA nodes, memory) and `pidlimit.Stats()`.
4. Constructs `CgroupManager` (v1 or v2, cgroupfs or systemd driver).
5. Validates `cgroupRoot` exists; appends `defaultNodeAllocatableCgroupName` (`kubepods`) to get the actual pod root.
6. Creates `QOSContainerManager`.
7. Creates `topologymanager.Manager`.
8. Creates `devicemanager.ManagerImpl`; registers it as a topology hint provider.
9. Optionally creates `dra.Manager`; registers DRA metrics collector.
10. Creates `cpumanager.Manager`; registers it as a topology hint provider.
11. Creates `memorymanager.Manager`; registers it as a topology hint provider.
12. Starts fan-in goroutines to multiplex update channels from device manager and DRA manager into a single `resourceUpdates` channel.

`ContainerManager.Start()` in `container_manager_linux.go:615`:

1. Builds `containerMap` from the CRI runtime to identify pre-existing containers.
2. Starts DRA manager.
3. Starts CPU manager (loads checkpoint, reconciles existing containers, starts reconcile loop).
4. Starts memory manager (same pattern).
5. Caches node info; fetches rootfs capacity from cAdvisor if local storage isolation is on.
6. Validates node allocatable configuration.
7. Calls `setupNode()`:
   a. Validates required cgroup subsystems exist (`validateSystemRequirements()`).
   b. Sets kernel tunables (`vm.overcommit_memory`, `vm.panic_on_oom`, `kernel.panic`, `kernel.panic_on_oops`, keyring limits) based on `ProtectKernelDefaults`.
   c. Creates and starts QOS container hierarchy if `CgroupsPerQOS`.
   d. Enforces node allocatable cgroups.
   e. Sets up `systemContainers` for the runtime and kubelet itself.
8. Starts periodic goroutines for `ensureStateFunc` (every 1 minute) and any periodic tasks (every 5 minutes).
9. Starts device manager (begins serving device plugin gRPC, loads checkpoints, reconciles containers).

### 3.2 Pod Admission Flow

When a new pod arrives and passes through kubelet's admit handlers (`kubelet.go:1059`):

```
lifecycle.PodAdmitHandler chain
└── containerManager.GetAllocateResourcesPodAdmitHandler()
    └── topologymanager.Manager.Admit()
        ├── For each container in pod:
        │   ├── cpuManager.GetTopologyHints()
        │   ├── memoryManager.GetTopologyHints()
        │   └── deviceManager.GetTopologyHints()
        ├── Merge hints → best NUMA affinity bitmask
        └── For each container:
            ├── cpuManager.Allocate()    → reserves CPUs in state
            ├── memoryManager.Allocate() → reserves NUMA memory blocks
            └── deviceManager.Allocate() → calls device plugin Allocate RPC
```

Additionally `lifecycle.NewPredicateAdmitHandler` calls `containerManager.UpdatePluginResources()`, which delegates to the device manager to update the scheduler's node info with current allocatable device capacity.

### 3.3 Container Launch Flow

When `kuberuntime` creates a container it calls through `InternalContainerLifecycle`:

```
kuberuntime.createContainer()
├── internalContainerLifecycle.PreCreateContainer()
│   ├── cpuManager.GetCPUAffinity() → inject cpuset.cpus into ContainerConfig
│   └── memoryManager.GetMemoryNUMANodes() → inject cpuset.mems into ContainerConfig
│
├── containerManager.GetResources()
│   ├── draManager.GetResources() → CDI device IDs for DRA
│   └── deviceManager.GetDeviceRunContainerOptions() → mounts, env vars, CDI devices
│       (allocation was already done at admission; this reads cached state)
│
├── CRI: CreateContainer(ContainerConfig with cpuset.cpus, cpuset.mems, devices, mounts)
│
└── internalContainerLifecycle.PreStartContainer()
    ├── cpuManager.AddContainer() → records containerID → (pod, container) mapping
    ├── memoryManager.AddContainer() → same
    └── topologyManager.AddContainer() → tracks for hint store
```

### 3.4 Pod Cgroup Lifecycle

Pod workers call `PodContainerManager.EnsureExists()` before running any containers:

```
podContainerManagerImpl.EnsureExists()
├── Determine QoS class → parent cgroup path
├── Build ResourceConfig from pod spec:
│   ├── ResourceConfigForPod() → CPU shares, CFS quota, memory limit
│   ├── PidsLimit if configured
│   └── MemoryQoS unified fields (memory.min, memory.high) if cgroup v2 + feature gate
└── cgroupManager.Create()
```

On pod termination, pod workers call `PodContainerManager.Destroy()`:

```
podContainerManagerImpl.Destroy()
├── tryKillingCgroupProcesses() → kill all PIDs in cgroup (up to 5 retries)
└── cgroupManager.Destroy()    → rmdir the cgroup
```

### 3.5 Device Health Update Loop

When a device plugin reports a device health change, the device manager sends to its internal `update` channel. A fan-in goroutine in `NewContainerManager` (`container_manager_linux.go:375`) forwards this to `cm.resourceUpdates`. The kubelet sync loop reads from `containerManager.Updates()` (`kubelet.go:2665`) and calls `handler.HandlePodSyncs()` for affected pods, causing a fresh status update that reflects the new device health.

---

## 4. Failure Modes, Limits, and Design Tradeoffs

### 4.1 Failure Modes

**Cgroup write failures on node allocatable enforcement**: `enforceNodeAllocatableCgroups()` (`node_container_manager_linux.go:95`) spawns a background goroutine that retries `cgroupManager.Update()` indefinitely with 1-minute sleeps, emitting Kubernetes Warning events on each failure. This prevents a hard startup failure when existing pod memory usage temporarily exceeds the new limit.

**QoS cgroup update failures**: `qosContainerManagerImpl.UpdateCgroups()` (`qos_container_manager_linux.go:316`) attempts a `retrySetMemoryReserve()` fallback that caps the limit to current memory usage when the first write is rejected. If updates still fail, the error is logged but the goroutine continues; QoS limits may drift from desired state until the next 1-minute reconcile.

**CPU manager checkpoint corruption**: If `state.NewCheckpointState()` fails, the CPU manager refuses to start and the kubelet returns an error. The operator must drain the node and remove the state file. This is intentional—silently starting with corrupt state would produce incorrect CPU pinning.

**Device plugin crashes**: The device manager marks devices from a crashed plugin as unhealthy and sends updates via the health channel. Pods using those devices get re-synced; the kubelet emits events. The checkpoint retains allocations so that if the plugin reconnects before the pod is deleted, allocation state can be reconciled.

**NUMA nodes > 8**: `topology_manager.go:41` defines `defaultMaxAllowableNUMANodes = 8`. Exceeding this causes `topologymanager.NewManager()` to return an error, preventing kubelet startup with topology manager enabled on large multi-socket machines.

**Swap enabled with `failSwapOn=true`**: `NewContainerManager` returns an error immediately, preventing kubelet from starting. With `failSwapOn=false`, the kubelet continues but emits a warning event about memory-backed volumes potentially being swapped to disk.

**cgroup v1 on new kernels**: A deprecation warning (`CgroupV1DeprecatedWarning`) is defined in `container_manager.go:52`. cgroup v1 support is intended for removal in a future release.

### 4.2 Design Tradeoffs

**Two-phase admission/allocation**: The topology manager's `Admit()` phase calls `Allocate()` on hint providers before the container is created. This means device allocations are committed at pod admission time, not at container start time. If the container never starts (e.g., image pull failure), the device remains allocated until the kubelet cleans up the pod.

**Checkpoint-based state persistence**: CPU, memory, and device managers all use checkpoint files under `KubeletRootDir`. This allows kubelet restarts without losing allocations. The downside is that checkpoint format changes require migration logic and draining.

**Synchronous QoS hierarchy updates**: The QoS cgroup update goroutine holds a mutex for its entire update cycle. During that time, new pod admissions that need `UpdateQOSCgroups()` must wait. This is acceptable because the update is fast (a few cgroup writes) and happens only once per minute.

**Noop implementations as a compile-time seam**: The `containerManagerStub` (`container_manager_stub.go`) and `podContainerManagerNoop` provide full no-op implementations of the interfaces. This decouples the kubelet's main path from the Linux-specific cgroup machinery at the cost of maintaining two parallel code paths.

---

## 5. Extension Points, Customisation Hooks, and Risks

### 5.1 CPU Manager Policy

`cpumanager.Policy` is an interface (`policy.go`) with `Start()`, `Allocate()`, `RemoveContainer()`, `GetTopologyHints()`, `GetAllocatableCPUs()`. New policies can be registered by adding a `case` to the `switch` in `NewManager()`. Policy options (`CPUManagerPolicyOptions map[string]string` in `NodeConfig`) allow feature flags per policy without changing the interface.

The static policy has options including `full-pcpus-only` (require whole physical cores), `distribute-cpus-across-numa`, `distribute-cpus-across-cores`, and `align-by-socket`. These are parsed in `policy_options.go` and change the CPU assignment topology.

**Risk**: A buggy policy can silently mis-assign CPUs or fail to clean up exclusive allocations; the CPU set leaked from a terminated container remains unavailable for new ones until the reconciler detects the container is gone.

### 5.2 Topology Manager Policy and Scope

Both `TopologyManagerPolicy` and `TopologyManagerScope` are strings from `NodeConfig`. Adding a new policy requires adding a `policy_*.go` file and registering it in `topology_manager.go`. The scope (container vs. pod) changes whether hints are merged per-container or across all containers at once.

**Risk**: `best-effort` silently allows non-optimal NUMA placement; operators may not notice increased cross-NUMA memory traffic until they measure latency. `restricted` and `single-numa-node` can cause admission failures that are opaque to end users if hint providers cannot agree.

### 5.3 Device Plugins

Device plugins register via the plugin watcher (`GetPluginRegistrationHandlers()` returns a map including `DevicePlugin` → `deviceManager.GetWatcherHandler()`). Any DaemonSet can register additional resource types without modifying kubelet code. The device manager's `Allocate()` delegates entirely to the plugin's gRPC response.

**Risk**: A device plugin that returns inconsistent topology information will cause the topology manager to make suboptimal NUMA placements. A plugin that crashes at `Allocate()` time causes pod admission to fail; kubelet will retry the admission on the next sync cycle.

### 5.4 DRA Drivers

DRA drivers register via the DRA plugin watcher (`DRAPlugin` key in `GetPluginRegistrationHandlers()`). The `dra.Manager` communicates with the driver to `PrepareResources()`, writing CDI device annotations that are passed to the CRI runtime. DRA is gated by `DynamicResourceAllocation` feature gate.

**Risk**: DRA state is stored in `ResourceClaim` objects in the API server and locally in the DRA manager's state directory. Inconsistencies between these two sources of truth on kubelet restart require the `PodMightNeedToUnprepareResources()` / `UnprepareResources()` recovery path; if the driver itself is unavailable at unprepare time, resources may leak.

### 5.5 MemoryQoS (cgroup v2 only)

Controlled by the `MemoryQoS` feature gate. When enabled, `memory.min` is set on QoS cgroups and pod cgroups to provide memory protection guarantees. The `Unified` map field in `ResourceConfig` carries arbitrary cgroup v2 key-value pairs, providing an extension point for future cgroup v2 features without changing the `ResourceConfig` struct.

### 5.6 PodLevelResources

The `qos_container_manager_linux.go:188` references `kubefeatures.PodLevelResources` to decide whether to skip pod-level resource aggregation when computing Burstable CPU shares. This is an example of a feature gate that changes the aggregation semantics within an existing component.

---

## 6. Operational Implications

### 6.1 Performance

**CPU manager reconciliation**: The reconcile loop calls `UpdateContainerResources()` on the CRI runtime for every container whose CPU set has changed since the last reconcile. On a node with hundreds of containers and the static policy, this can produce bursts of gRPC calls. The reconcile period (`CPUManagerReconcilePeriod`, default 10 seconds) controls this tradeoff.

**QoS cgroup update cost**: Every minute, `UpdateCgroups()` iterates all active pods to sum CPU requests. On nodes with many pods, this is O(pods × containers). The update itself is a handful of cgroup writes, but the pod iteration under lock can delay admission.

**Cgroup write latency on systemd**: When using the systemd cgroup driver, `cgroup_manager_linux.go` converts every cgroup name to a systemd slice path. Systemd must be notified via D-Bus for each cgroup operation. On high-density nodes this can become a bottleneck; the cgroupfs driver avoids this overhead.

### 6.2 Reliability

**Kubelet restart resilience**: CPU, memory, and device managers write state to checkpoint files before returning success from `Allocate()`. On restart, `Start()` reads the checkpoint and calls `UpdateContainerResources()` for any container still running to restore affinity. This means brief affinity drift (the container runs on the wrong CPUs) during the window between kubelet crash and restart recovery.

**cgroup hierarchy divergence**: The QoS and pod container managers are responsible for creating and destroying cgroups in sync with pod lifecycle. If the kubelet crashes between creating a pod cgroup and launching the container, orphan cgroups may exist. `GetAllPodsFromCgroups()` on `podContainerManagerImpl` scans the filesystem to enumerate orphan pod cgroups, which `HandlePodCleanups()` uses for garbage collection.

**`ShouldResetExtendedResourceCapacity()`**: Called on kubelet startup via `kubelet_resources.go`. The device manager returns `true` if the checkpoint indicates the node identity changed (e.g., node was recreated). In that case the kubelet zeroes extended resource capacity in the node status, forcing device plugins to re-register.

### 6.3 Maintainability

**cgroup v1 deprecation**: `container_manager.go:52` contains the deprecation warning. The v1-specific code paths in `cgroup_v1_manager_linux.go` and the v1 branches in `helpers_linux.go` represent ongoing maintenance burden. Removal requires coordinated changes across multiple files.

**`klog.TODO()` debt**: Several methods in `container_manager_linux.go` (e.g., `PodHasExclusiveCPUs`, `GetCapacity`) call `klog.TODO()` because they lack a proper context. This is flagged in source comments as a known issue requiring refactoring to pass loggers through more of the call chain.

**Mockery-generated mocks**: The `//go:generate mockery` directive in `container_manager.go` generates mocks for testing. The `.mockery.yaml` controls which interfaces are mocked. This keeps test doubles in sync with the interface, but any interface change requires running `go generate` and committing updated mock files.

---

## 7. File and Directory Map

| Path | Role |
|------|------|
| `pkg/kubelet/cm/container_manager.go` | `ContainerManager` interface, `NodeConfig`, `NodeAllocatableConfig`, helper functions |
| `pkg/kubelet/cm/container_manager_linux.go` | Linux `containerManagerImpl`: construction, `Start()`, resource delegation |
| `pkg/kubelet/cm/container_manager_windows.go` | Windows `containerManagerImpl`: capacity and device management only |

| `pkg/kubelet/cm/cgroup_v1_manager_linux.go` | cgroup v1 implementation (`cgroupV1impl`) |
| `pkg/kubelet/cm/cgroup_v2_manager_linux.go` | cgroup v2 implementation (`cgroupV2impl`) |
